name: Separation in Time
description: Separate conflicting requirements by performing them at different times.
techniques: Perform the conflicting actions at different time intervals
Alternate between the two states periodically
Use pre-action: prepare in advance when the conflict is absent
Use post-action: perform the action after the main process
examples: Traffic lights alternate red and green phases to resolve the contradiction of crossing flows
Expandable luggage that is compact for storage and expands when needed
Pre-stressed concrete: tension applied before load to handle both compression and tension
Seasonal tires swapped between summer and winter for different grip requirements

name: Separation in Space
description: Separate conflicting requirements by placing them in different locations.
techniques: Place the conflicting properties in different spatial zones
Use a gradient of properties across the object
Move the contradictory requirement to an adjacent system
Use nested or layered structures with different properties per layer
examples: A pencil is hard at the tip (for writing) and soft at the eraser end (for erasing)
Aircraft wing: rigid spar for strength, flexible skin for aerodynamics
Multi-layer PCB with signal layers separated from power/ground planes
Graded-index optical fiber: refractive index varies from center to edge

name: Separation in Scale (Between Parts and Whole)
description: Resolve the contradiction by having the whole exhibit one property while individual parts exhibit the opposite.
techniques: Use many small elements that individually have one property but collectively exhibit the opposite
Introduce porosity or voids at micro-scale while maintaining macro-scale solidity
Apply hierarchical structures where each level has different behavior
Use composite materials with different properties at different scales
examples: Chain mail: each link is rigid, but the whole is flexible
Foam: solid material at micro-scale, compressible at macro-scale
Carbon fiber composite: brittle fibers woven into a tough, flexible sheet
Pixel displays: discrete red/green/blue elements appear as continuous color at viewing distance

name: Separation Upon Condition
description: Make the system exhibit different properties depending on operating conditions.
techniques: Use phase transitions triggered by temperature, pressure, or other conditions
Apply smart materials that change properties in response to stimuli
Use chemical reactions that are reversible under different conditions
Employ adaptive mechanisms that switch behavior based on sensor input
examples: Shape-memory alloy: deformable at low temperature, returns to original shape when heated
Dilatant fluid (oobleck): liquid under gentle force, solid under impact
Photochromic lenses: transparent indoors, darken in sunlight
Self-healing polymers: stable normally, flow to fill cracks when damaged
